walkthrough.title: Unfold the Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Pandemic: Fall of Rome set up. Unfold the game board and place it in the center of the table. You will see a map of the Roman Empire with cities connected by roads and sea routes. There are five colored migration paths entering from the edges of the board, one for each barbarian tribe. Take a moment to notice the Invasion Rate track in the upper right and the Decline track along the bottom. Both of these will matter a lot during the game.

walkthrough.title: Place the Fort in Roma
walkthrough.text: Find the six fort tokens. These are the small wooden fortress pieces. Place one fort in Roma, right in the center of the map. Roma starts fortified because it is the heart of the Empire and the city you must protect above all else. Set the other five forts aside near the board. You will build more forts during the game, but you only have six total, so use them wisely.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Supply
walkthrough.text: Gather all 16 legion tokens. These are the small Roman soldier pieces. Place them in a supply pile near the board where everyone can reach them. Next, sort the barbarian cubes by color: orange for Anglo-Saxons and Franks, black for Vandals, green for Huns, yellow for Visigoths, and blue for Ostrogoths. Place each color in its own supply area near the edge of the board. Also place the Decline marker on the starting space of the Decline track and the Invasion Rate marker on the starting position.

walkthrough.title: Separate the Card Decks
walkthrough.text: There are three types of cards to sort. First, the Player deck cards, which include City cards and Event cards. Set these aside for now. Second, the Barbarian cards, which have gold borders and show barbarian invasion routes. Set these aside too. Third, the Revolt cards, which you will shuffle into the Player deck later. Finally, find the Role cards and Reference cards and set them in a separate pile.

walkthrough.title: Initial Invasion Setup
walkthrough.text: This is the big one. Shuffle the Barbarian deck and flip five cards face up. For each card, find the city shown and check the tribe color. Discard these five cards without placing any cubes. These are the Roma cards being removed. Now draw nine more Barbarian cards with gold borders. Draw the first three cards and place three cubes of the matching tribe color on each city shown. Draw the next three and place two cubes each. Draw the final three and place one cube each. That is nine cities seeded with barbarians in a 3-2-1 pattern, just like original Pandemic. Place all nine cards face up in a discard pile.

walkthrough.title: Deal Role Cards
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the Role cards and deal one face down to each player. Everyone flips their role over and reads their special ability. Each role gives you a unique power that breaks the normal rules in some way. Take the matching colored pawn for your role. Also give each player a Reference card summarizing the actions available on your turn. Keep your role visible to all players throughout the game since this is a fully cooperative game with open information.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Hands
walkthrough.text: Take the City cards and Event cards and shuffle them together. This will become the Player deck, but first deal starting hands. With two players, deal four cards each. With three players, deal three cards each. With four players, deal two cards each. With five players, deal two cards each. Everyone can look at their cards and share information openly. Set the remaining cards aside for the next step.

walkthrough.title: Build the Player Deck with Revolt Cards
walkthrough.text: Now we add the Revolt cards to the Player deck. Choose your difficulty: Introductory uses five Revolt cards, Standard uses six, and Heroic uses seven. Divide the remaining Player cards into as many roughly equal face-down piles as the number of Revolt cards you are using. Shuffle one Revolt card into each pile without looking. Then stack the piles on top of each other to form the final Player deck. This ensures Revolt cards are spread throughout the game rather than clumping together.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Pawns and Legions
walkthrough.text: Each player looks at their starting hand and picks one card that shows a city. Place your pawn in that city along with two legions from the supply. If multiple players choose the same city, that is fine. You all start there together. Choose strategically based on where the barbarian threats are heaviest from the initial invasion setup.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Alliance Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the five Alliance cards, one for each barbarian tribe. Place them near the board where everyone can see them. When you forge an alliance with a tribe during the game, you will flip that tribe's Alliance card to its allied side. This is how you track which tribes have been dealt with diplomatically.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Battle Dice
walkthrough.text: Place the three battle dice near the board where everyone can reach them. These custom dice have special faces showing different combat outcomes. You will roll these when fighting barbarians during the game. Also place the Barbarian deck near the board with room for a discard pile.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: The player whose starting city is farthest from Roma goes first. If there is a tie, the group decides. Play proceeds clockwise from the first player. You are Roman leaders working together to save the Empire. Ready to hold back the barbarian hordes? Let's learn the rules.

Set remaining 5 forts aside (6 total in the game)

Divide remaining Player cards into equal piles

Place your pawn + 2 legions from supply in that city

summary.title: Determine First Player
bullets: Player whose starting city is farthest from Roma goes first
Play proceeds clockwise

walkthrough.title: Turn Overview
walkthrough.text: Each turn has three phases, always in the same order. First, you take four actions. You can mix and match from the available action types, and you can repeat the same action multiple times. Second, you draw two Player cards from the top of the deck. If you draw a Revolt card, you resolve it immediately. Third, barbarians invade. You flip a number of Barbarian cards equal to the current invasion rate and add cubes to the board. After the third phase, play passes clockwise to the next player.

walkthrough.title: March
walkthrough.text: March lets you move your pawn to an adjacent city connected by a road. When you march, you can bring up to three legions from your current city with you. You can also pick up legions along the way if you move through cities that have them. Marching is your primary way to get around the map and reposition your forces. Remember, you can march multiple times in a single turn to cover more ground, spending one action per move.

walkthrough.title: Sail
walkthrough.text: Sailing lets you move between port cities across the sea. To sail, you must discard a Player card that matches your current city or your destination city. Port cities are marked with an anchor symbol on the board. Sailing is powerful because it lets you cross the map quickly, but it costs a card, so use it when you really need to cover long distances fast. You can bring legions with you when you sail, just like marching.

walkthrough.title: Fortify
walkthrough.text: To fortify a city, discard a Player card that matches the city you are currently in and place a fort token there. Each city can hold at most one fort, and there are only six forts in the entire game. Forts are critical because they let you recruit armies and they protect your legions from ambush. A city with a fort and either a pawn or legions is much harder for barbarians to overrun. Choose your fort locations carefully since you cannot move them once placed.

walkthrough.title: Recruit Army
walkthrough.text: You can only recruit in a city that has a fort. Spend one action to add legions from the supply equal to the city's recruitment value. The number you recruit depends on the city's population, shown on the board. Recruiting is how you replenish your military forces, and since there are only 16 legions total in the supply, you need to manage them carefully. If the supply runs out, you cannot recruit more until legions are freed up.

walkthrough.title: Battle
walkthrough.text: When you are in a city with barbarian cubes, you can battle them. Roll up to one die per legion in the city, to a maximum of three dice. The custom dice have several faces: a legion icon means you lose one legion back to supply, a barbarian icon means you remove one barbarian cube, a combined icon means you remove one barbarian but also lose one legion, and a double-remove face removes two barbarians but costs one legion. Some role cards have a special die face that triggers their unique combat ability. Combat is risky but necessary to clear the board.

walkthrough.title: Plot
walkthrough.text: Plot lets you share cards with another player who is in the same city as you. You can either give a card to that player or take a card from them. The card exchanged must match the city you are both currently in. This is essential for gathering the sets of matching-color cards you need to forge alliances. Coordinate with your teammates to pass the right cards to whoever is closest to completing an alliance. Remember the hand limit of seven cards applies at all times.

walkthrough.title: Forge Alliance
walkthrough.text: This is one of the two ways to deal with a barbarian tribe permanently. Discard a set of Player cards all matching one tribe's color to forge an alliance. The number needed depends on the tribe: Anglo-Saxons and Franks require five, Vandals require five, Huns require four, Visigoths require four, and Ostrogoths require only three. When you forge an alliance, flip that tribe's Alliance card to its allied side. Allied barbarians still invade and place cubes, but you gain a powerful new action: Enlist Barbarians.

walkthrough.title: Enlist Barbarians
walkthrough.text: After you forge an alliance with a tribe, you unlock the Enlist Barbarians action for that tribe's color. When you are in a city with cubes of an allied tribe, discard one card matching that tribe's color to remove all cubes of that color from the city. For each cube removed, add one legion to that city from the supply. This is incredibly powerful. You are converting barbarian invaders into Roman soldiers. It is often more efficient than battling, especially in cities overrun with a single tribe's cubes.

walkthrough.title: Draw Player Cards
walkthrough.text: After taking your four actions, draw the top two cards from the Player deck. These can be City cards, which match specific cities and tribes, or Event cards, which give you powerful one-time abilities. If you draw a Revolt card, stop immediately and resolve it. A Revolt card triggers three things in order: First, the invasion rate increases by one step. Second, the bottom card of the Barbarian discard pile revolts, and you place three cubes of that tribe's color in that city. Third, you shuffle the entire Barbarian discard pile and place it on top of the Barbarian deck. This is called Intensify, and it guarantees that recently invaded cities will be hit again soon.

walkthrough.title: Invade Cities
walkthrough.text: After drawing cards, flip Barbarian cards equal to the current invasion rate. For each card, add one cube of the matching tribe color to the city shown. However, barbarian invasion follows migration paths. Cubes can only be placed if the pictured city already has cubes of that color, or if a city earlier on that tribe's migration path has cubes. If neither condition is met, the cube is placed at the start of the migration path instead. After placing each cube, discard that Barbarian card face up.

walkthrough.title: Defending Cities
walkthrough.text: When a barbarian cube would be placed in a city that has legions, there are two possible outcomes depending on fortification. If the city has a fort or a player pawn, the legions are attacked: remove one legion and do not place the barbarian cube. Your soldiers held the line. If the city has legions but no fort and no player pawn, the legions are ambushed: remove all legions from that city and still place the barbarian cube. Ambush is devastating. This is why forts are so important. Always try to have a fort or a player pawn in any city where you station legions.

walkthrough.title: Sacking
walkthrough.text: If a fourth cube of the same color would ever be placed in a single city, that city is sacked. The cube is not placed. Instead, move the Decline marker down one space on the Decline track, remove any fort in that city, and spread one cube of that tribe's color to each adjacent city. This spreading can trigger chain reactions. If an adjacent city also reaches four cubes of the same color from the spread, it gets sacked too. Chain sacking can be catastrophic and spiral out of control quickly. Preventing sacks is one of the most important strategic priorities.

walkthrough.title: Winning and Losing
walkthrough.text: You win when all five barbarian tribes have been resolved. A tribe is resolved when it is either allied with, meaning you forged an alliance using matching cards, or eliminated, meaning there are zero cubes of that color anywhere on the board. You can mix and match. You lose if any of four things happen: Roma is sacked, the Decline track reaches the final space, the Player deck runs out when you need to draw, or you cannot place a barbarian cube because the supply of that color is exhausted. Keep all four loss conditions in mind throughout the game.

bullets: Phase 1: Take 4 actions (can repeat same action)
Phase 2: Draw 2 Player cards (resolve Revolt cards immediately)
Phase 3: Invade — flip Barbarian cards equal to invasion rate

bullets: Win: all 5 tribes allied or eliminated (no cubes on board)
Lose: Roma sacked, Decline track full, Player deck empty, or barbarian cube supply exhausted

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Tutorial
walkthrough.text: We are going to walk through the first two turns of a four-player game. Our players are Player 1, Player 2, Player 3, and Player 4, each with a different role. The board is set up with barbarians from the initial invasion, Roma is fortified, and each player has their pawn and two legions in a starting city. Follow along even if your group has fewer players. The goal is to see every major mechanic in action before you play on your own.

walkthrough.title: The Starting Situation
walkthrough.text: Take a look at the board. The initial invasion placed barbarians across the Empire. There are three cities with heavy concentrations of three cubes each, three cities with two cubes, and three with one cube. Roma has its fort but no barbarian cubes yet. Player 1 starts in a city near the northern border with two legions. Player 2 is on the eastern frontier. Player 3 is near the southern coast. Player 4 is closest to Roma.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: March with Legions
walkthrough.text: Player 1 starts their turn with four actions. For the first action, Player 1 marches to an adjacent city that has two orange Anglo-Saxon cubes. They bring both of their legions along. Remember, when you march you can take up to three legions with you. Player 1 wants to deal with those barbarians before they spread further.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Battle the Barbarians
walkthrough.text: For the second action, Player 1 battles. They have two legions in the city, so they roll two dice. The first die shows a barbarian icon — one orange cube is removed from the board and returned to the supply. The second die shows a combined icon — another barbarian cube is removed, but Player 1 also loses one legion back to the supply. The city is now clear of barbarians, but Player 1 is down to one legion. Combat is effective but costly.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Remaining Actions
walkthrough.text: With two actions left, Player 1 marches again to another adjacent city and then uses a fourth action to march one more step along the northern border. Player 1 is positioning to deal with more barbarian threats next turn. Notice how four actions goes quickly when you are covering ground across the map. Planning your route before you start moving is important.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Draw Cards and Invade
walkthrough.text: Player 1 draws two Player cards from the deck. Both are City cards, which is good. Now the invasion phase begins. The invasion rate starts at two, so Player 1 flips two Barbarian cards. The first card shows a city on the green Hun migration path. A green cube is placed there. The second card shows a city on the yellow Visigoth path with existing yellow cubes, so one more yellow cube is added. The barbarian pressure builds every turn.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Attempting to Recruit
walkthrough.text: Player 2 wants to recruit legions because they only have two left and the eastern frontier is under heavy pressure. They announce they want to use the Recruit Army action. But wait — Player 2's city does not have a fort. You can only recruit in a city with a fort. This is a common mistake new players make. Player 2 needs to either move to a city with a fort or build one first.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Recruiting Requires a Fort
walkthrough.text: Remember this rule: you cannot recruit legions unless there is a fort in your city. Roma starts with a fort, but everywhere else you need to build one using the Fortify action, which costs a matching city card. Plan your fortification early. If you wait until you are desperate for legions, it is already too late. The best players build forts in key cities during the first few turns.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Fortify Instead
walkthrough.text: Player 2 adjusts their plan. They have a card matching their current city, so they discard it and use the Fortify action to place a fort. Now this city has a fort. For their second action, Player 2 recruits legions. The city has a recruitment value of two, so Player 2 takes two legions from the supply and places them in the city. Now Player 2 has four legions here and can defend the eastern frontier.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Battle on the Eastern Front
walkthrough.text: Player 2 spends their third action to march into a nearby city with three blue Ostrogoth cubes, bringing three legions along. For the fourth action, Player 2 battles. With three legions they roll three dice. Results: one barbarian removed, one combined result removing a barbarian and losing a legion, and one legion icon losing another legion. Two Ostrogoth cubes removed but two legions lost. The city still has one blue cube remaining. Combat is a war of attrition.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Draw and Invade
walkthrough.text: Player 2 draws two Player cards, getting one City card and one Event card. Events are powerful one-time effects you can play on any player's turn. During the invasion phase, two more Barbarian cards flip. One adds a black Vandal cube to a southern city. The other adds an orange cube to a city on the Anglo-Saxon migration path. The threats are spreading across multiple fronts.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: Moving Legions Without a Pawn
walkthrough.text: Player 3 wants to send legions from their starting city to help Player 2. But here is a critical mistake: you cannot move legions independently. Legions only move when a player pawn marches or sails and takes them along. Legions left behind in a city without a pawn or fort are vulnerable to ambush. Player 3 learns that they need to personally escort their legions to where they are needed.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Legions Follow Your Pawn
walkthrough.text: This is an important concept. Legions are not autonomous. They travel with you when you march or sail, and they stay put when you leave them behind. If you leave legions in a city with no fort and no player pawn, they are vulnerable to ambush — meaning if barbarians invade that city, all your legions there are destroyed instead of just one. Always leave legions in fortified cities, or take them with you.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: March and Recruit at Roma
walkthrough.text: Player 3 adjusts their plan. They march toward Roma, bringing both legions. For their second action, Player 3 recruits at Roma since it has a fort. They gain legions equal to Roma's recruitment value. Now Player 3 has a solid force. They use their remaining two actions to march south toward the coast where black Vandal cubes are accumulating. Player 3 brings three legions along to confront the southern threat.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: Draw Cards — A Revolt Card Appears
walkthrough.text: Player 3 draws their first Player card — it is a City card. But the second card is a Revolt card. Trouble. When you draw a Revolt card, three things happen immediately in this order. First, the Invasion Rate marker moves up one step, so future turns will flip more Barbarian cards. Second, take the bottom card of the Barbarian discard pile and place three cubes of that tribe's color in the city shown — this is the Revolt itself. Third, shuffle the entire Barbarian discard pile and place it on top of the Barbarian deck. This is Intensify, and it means every city that was recently invaded is about to be invaded again.

walkthrough.title: Understanding the Revolt Impact
walkthrough.text: Take a moment to process what just happened. The invasion rate increased from two to three, meaning three Barbarian cards will flip every invasion phase from now on. A city just received three cubes from the Revolt, putting it dangerously close to sacking. And all those recently discarded Barbarian cards are now shuffled back on top of the deck, so you know exactly which cities are about to be hit again. This last part is actually useful information if you pay attention. You can predict where the next invasions will land and position your legions accordingly.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: Invasion with Migration Path
walkthrough.text: Now Player 3 resolves the invasion phase, flipping three Barbarian cards because the invasion rate just went up. The first card shows a city deep on the Hun migration path but there are no green cubes on the board there or on earlier path cities. The cube goes to the starting city of the Hun migration path instead. The second card adds a yellow Visigoth cube to a city that already has two yellow cubes — now it has three, dangerously close to sacking. The third card reinforces an existing orange position. Migration paths determine where cubes actually land.

walkthrough.title: Player 4: Sharing Cards via Plot
walkthrough.text: Player 4 is near Roma and notices that Player 3 just marched through a city where Player 4 is located. Player 4 has been collecting blue Ostrogoth cards and needs one more to forge an alliance. Player 3 happens to have a blue card. Player 4 uses the Plot action to take the blue card from Player 3. Remember, the card exchanged in a Plot must match the city both players are currently in. This is why meeting up in the right city matters.

walkthrough.title: Player 4: Working Toward an Alliance
walkthrough.text: Player 4 now has the cards needed to forge an alliance with the Ostrogoths. But they need three matching blue cards and they want to wait for the right moment. For now, Player 4 uses their remaining three actions to march toward the eastern front where Ostrogoth cubes are building up. If Player 4 can forge the alliance next turn, they can immediately start enlisting those blue cubes as legions.

walkthrough.title: Player 4: Draw and Invade
walkthrough.text: Player 4 draws two Player cards, both City cards. During invasion, three Barbarian cards flip. One of them targets a city with three yellow Visigoth cubes. A fourth yellow cube would trigger sacking. But the city has Player 3's legions and Player 3 is present with their pawn. This means the legions are attacked, not ambushed. One legion is removed but the cube is not placed. The defense held. The other two invasions add cubes to less threatened areas. Player 3's positioning just prevented a sack.

walkthrough.title: Turn 2: Player 1 Forges Ahead
walkthrough.text: The second round begins. Player 1 has been collecting orange Anglo-Saxon cards and decides to battle the remaining barbarians in a northern city before pushing further. They march, battle, and clear another pocket of orange cubes. Every cube removed from the board brings you closer to eliminating a tribe. Player 1 draws cards and resolves invasions. The game's rhythm is becoming clear: move, fight, draw, invade, repeat.

walkthrough.title: Turn 2: Player 2 Recruits and Repositions
walkthrough.text: Player 2 marches back to their fortified city and recruits more legions. The supply is getting low — remember, there are only 16 legions total. Player 2 spends their remaining actions fortifying a second key city on the blue Ostrogoth migration path. This creates a defensive line that will slow the barbarian advance and give the team more options for recruiting.

walkthrough.title: Turn 2: Player 4 Forges the Ostrogoth Alliance
walkthrough.text: Player 4 has three blue Ostrogoth cards. The Ostrogoths only require three cards for an alliance, the cheapest of all five tribes. Player 4 discards the three cards and flips the Ostrogoth Alliance card to its allied side. The Ostrogoths are now allied. Blue cubes will still appear during invasions, but Player 4 and all other players can now use the Enlist Barbarians action on blue cubes. First alliance forged. Four tribes to go.

walkthrough.title: Turn 2: Enlisting Barbarians in Action
walkthrough.text: Player 4 is in a city with two blue Ostrogoth cubes. They discard one blue card and use the Enlist Barbarians action. Both blue cubes are removed from the city, and Player 4 gains two legions from the supply in their place. Those barbarians just became Roman soldiers. This is the power of alliances. Instead of losing legions in risky combat, you convert enemy cubes directly into your own forces. The team is starting to turn the tide.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen all the core mechanics in action. You watched legions march and battle barbarians. You saw what happens when you try to recruit without a fort and when legions are left without protection. You experienced a Revolt card and saw how the Intensify shuffle changes the threat landscape. You witnessed an alliance being forged and barbarians being enlisted as legions. You understand the rhythm of the game: act, draw, invade. You are ready to defend the Empire on your own.

walkthrough.title: Before You March On
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your own game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategic priorities and common mistakes to avoid.

Second — reference the Appendix for quick definitions of roles, actions, tribes, and combat mechanics.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Ave Roma!

Follow along even with fewer players

Legions left alone without fort/pawn are vulnerable to ambush

summary.title: Lesson: Legions Follow Your Pawn
bullets: Legions travel only when a player marches or sails with them
Unprotected legions (no fort, no pawn) get ambushed = all destroyed
Leave legions in fortified cities or take them with you

summary.title: Player 3: March and Recruit at Roma
bullets: March to Roma, recruit using Roma's fort
Build a strong force then march south toward Vandal threat
Bring 3 legions along to confront barbarians

summary.title: Player 3: Draw Cards — A Revolt Card Appears
bullets: Revolt card triggers 3 effects: increase invasion rate, revolt bottom discard (3 cubes), Intensify shuffle
Invasion rate goes from 2 to 3 — more pressure every turn
Recently invaded cities will be hit again soon

summary.title: Understanding the Revolt Impact
bullets: Invasion rate increased permanently
One city just received 3 cubes (near sacking)
Discarded Barbarian cards shuffled on top = predictable threats

summary.title: Player 3: Invasion with Migration Path
bullets: 3 Barbarian cards flipped (new invasion rate)
Cubes follow migration paths — no existing cubes = placed at path start
Migration paths restrict where tribes can spread

summary.title: Player 4: Sharing Cards via Plot
bullets: Plot: give or take a card with another player in your city
Card must match the city both players are in
Meeting in the right city enables card sharing for alliances

summary.title: Player 4: Working Toward an Alliance
bullets: Collecting matching tribe cards toward an alliance
Ostrogoths need only 3 cards — cheapest alliance
Positioning near Ostrogoth cubes to enlist after alliance

summary.title: Player 4: Draw and Invade
bullets: Invasion targets city with 3 yellow cubes (would be 4th = sack)
Player 3's legions + pawn = attacked, not ambushed
Lose 1 legion but cube is NOT placed — defense prevented a sack

summary.title: Turn 2: Player 1 Forges Ahead
bullets: March, battle, clear another pocket of barbarians
Every cube removed brings you closer to eliminating a tribe
Game rhythm: move, fight, draw, invade

summary.title: Turn 2: Player 2 Recruits and Repositions
bullets: Return to fortified city to recruit more legions
Only 16 legions total — supply management is critical
Fortify a second city to create a defensive line

summary.title: Turn 2: Player 4 Forges the Ostrogoth Alliance
bullets: Discard 3 blue Ostrogoth cards to forge alliance
Ostrogoths need only 3 cards (cheapest tribe)
Blue cubes still invade but can now be enlisted

summary.title: Turn 2: Enlisting Barbarians in Action
bullets: Discard 1 blue card in city with blue cubes
Remove ALL blue cubes, gain 1 legion per cube removed
Converts enemies into soldiers — more efficient than combat

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: You've seen marching, battling, recruiting, fortifying, plotting, forging alliances, and enlisting
Watched common mistakes: recruiting without a fort, leaving legions unprotected
Experienced a Revolt card and migration path mechanics

summary.title: Before You March On
bullets: Check out General Tips for strategic priorities
Reference the Appendix for quick definitions of all game terms
Ask your personal GM Guide — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Protect Roma Above All
walkthrough.text: Roma being sacked is an instant loss, no matter how well you are doing everywhere else. Always keep Roma in mind. If barbarian cubes start appearing in cities adjacent to Roma, deal with them immediately. A single chain reaction of sacking events can cascade cubes into Roma before you can react. Keep the Roma fort intact and try to maintain at least one or two legions there at all times. It is the single most important city on the board.

walkthrough.title: Fortify Key Cities on Migration Paths
walkthrough.text: Forts are your best defensive tool, but you only have six. Do not waste them on remote cities. Instead, place forts at chokepoints where migration paths converge or pass through heavily trafficked areas. A fort at a hub city can protect multiple routes at once. Ideal fort locations are cities that sit on multiple migration paths, because defending one city there effectively slows down two or three tribes. Roma already has a fort, so you really only have five to place strategically.

walkthrough.title: Coordinate Card Sharing for Alliances
walkthrough.text: Forging alliances is the most reliable way to deal with barbarian tribes. But gathering five matching cards, or even three for the Ostrogoths, requires teamwork. Use the Plot action to transfer cards between players when you meet in the right city. Designate one player as the alliance builder for each tribe and funnel matching cards to them. Do not spread your card collection across three players. Concentrate cards in one hand for the fastest alliance.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Spread Too Thin
walkthrough.text: It is tempting to send one player to every trouble spot on the map, but spreading your forces thin means you are weak everywhere. Instead, focus your efforts. Let two players work one side of the map while the others handle the opposite side. Concentrate your legions rather than scattering them in ones and twos. Three legions in one city can roll three dice and fight effectively. One legion alone is barely worth the action to move it there.

walkthrough.title: Manage the Legion Supply
walkthrough.text: There are only 16 legions in the entire game. Every legion lost in battle or ambush goes back to the supply and must be recruited again. If you are losing legions faster than you can recruit them, you are in trouble. Avoid unnecessary battles where the odds are poor. Do not leave legions in unfortified cities where they can be ambushed. And always keep an eye on the supply count. Running out of legions means you have nothing to defend with.

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Which Tribes to Deal With First
walkthrough.text: Not all tribes are equal. The Ostrogoths only need three cards for an alliance — that is the cheapest deal on the board. Start there. Once you have one alliance, the Enlist Barbarians action becomes available for that color, giving you a powerful tool. After the Ostrogoths, the Huns and Visigoths at four cards each are the next most efficient. Anglo-Saxons/Franks and Vandals at five cards each take the longest, so plan for those to be resolved through combat or late-game alliances.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Decline Track
walkthrough.text: The Decline track is the timer on your game. Every time a city is sacked, the marker drops one step. If it reaches the end, you lose. But sacking also causes chain reactions that can drop it multiple steps at once. You might think you have plenty of room, and then a single bad invasion triggers three sacks in a row. Always leave yourself a buffer. If the Decline track is getting low, prioritize preventing sacks over everything else. A sack-prevention play is worth more than an offensive push.

walkthrough.title: Event Cards Are Powerful But Risky
walkthrough.text: Event cards come in two types. Standard Events have a straightforward beneficial effect, like moving extra legions or removing cubes. Use these whenever the situation calls for it. Corrupt Events are printed upside-down and give you a choice: you can use the powerful effect, but at a cost, or you can ignore it. The corrupt option might remove cubes from one area but add them to another, or it might cost legions. Read both options carefully and weigh the tradeoff. Sometimes the corrupt version is worth the cost, but sometimes it makes things worse.

walkthrough.title: Use the Barbarian Discard Pile as Intel
walkthrough.text: After the Intensify shuffle from a Revolt card, the recently discarded Barbarian cards are now on top of the deck. This means you know exactly which cities will be invaded next. Use this knowledge to position your legions defensively. If you know a city with three cubes is about to be hit again, get a defender there before it sacks. After the shuffled cards are drawn through, the deck returns to unknown territory. Pay attention to the discard pile and you will be one step ahead of the barbarians.

walkthrough.title: Timing Your Alliances
walkthrough.text: Forging an alliance at the right moment can swing the game. The best time is when there are many cubes of that tribe's color scattered across the board. Every Enlist Barbarians action after the alliance converts cubes into legions, so more cubes on the board means more potential soldiers. Do not rush an alliance when there are only one or two cubes of that color. Wait until the board is saturated, then forge the alliance and enlist aggressively to swing the balance in your favor.

summary.title: Protect Roma Above All
bullets: Roma sacked = instant loss regardless of other progress
Keep legions and the fort in Roma at all times
Watch for chain sacking cascading toward Roma

summary.title: Fortify Key Cities on Migration Paths
bullets: Only 6 forts total (1 starts in Roma)
Place forts at chokepoints where migration paths converge
A well-placed fort slows multiple tribes at once

summary.title: Coordinate Card Sharing for Alliances
bullets: Use Plot to transfer cards between players in the same city
Designate one player per tribe as the alliance builder
Concentrate cards — don't spread across multiple players

summary.title: Do Not Spread Too Thin
bullets: Concentrate forces rather than scattering legions
3 legions together fight effectively; 1 alone is weak
Assign players to specific regions of the map

summary.title: Manage the Legion Supply
bullets: Only 16 legions total — lost legions return to supply
Avoid unnecessary battles and ambush situations
Running out of legions leaves you defenseless

summary.title: Prioritize Which Tribes to Deal With First
bullets: Ostrogoths: 3 cards (cheapest, do first)
Huns / Visigoths: 4 cards each
Anglo-Saxons/Franks / Vandals: 5 cards each (hardest)

summary.title: Watch the Decline Track
bullets: Decline track reaching the end = you lose
Chain sacking can drop it multiple steps at once
Always leave a buffer — prevention beats offense

summary.title: Event Cards Are Powerful But Risky
bullets: Standard Events: straightforward beneficial effects
Corrupt Events: powerful but come with a cost
Read both options carefully before choosing

summary.title: Use the Barbarian Discard Pile as Intel
bullets: After Intensify, you know which cities are on top of the deck
Position legions defensively based on this knowledge
Predict invasions and prevent sacking before it happens

summary.title: Timing Your Alliances
bullets: Best time to forge: when many cubes of that color are on the board
More cubes = more legions gained through Enlist Barbarians
Don't rush alliances with only 1-2 cubes out

walkthrough.title: Optimal Fortification Placement
walkthrough.text: Your five deployable forts should go on cities that serve multiple purposes: they sit on migration paths, have good recruitment values, and connect to many other cities. Avoid fortifying dead-end cities on the map edge. The ideal fort city is one that blocks two or more migration paths, has a recruitment value of at least two, and connects to three or more adjacent cities so your legions can respond to threats in multiple directions. Think of forts as military bases, not walls. They are launch points for counterattacks.

walkthrough.title: Alliance Priority Order
walkthrough.text: The optimal alliance order in most games is Ostrogoths first because they only cost three cards, then either Huns or Visigoths at four cards depending on which tribe is causing more trouble. Save Anglo-Saxons/Franks and Vandals for last since they cost five cards each. However, sometimes a tribe is about to overwhelm the board and you need to forge their alliance immediately regardless of cost. Flexibility matters, but in a vacuum, always go cheapest first. Each alliance gives you the Enlist action which provides exponentially growing returns.

walkthrough.title: When to Battle vs When to Enlist
walkthrough.text: Battle is best when you have plenty of legions, the cube count is low, and you need to clear cubes of a non-allied tribe. The expected value of three dice is roughly one to two cubes removed with one legion lost. Enlisting is vastly more efficient: one card removes all cubes of an allied color from a city and converts them into legions. As a rule, if you have an alliance, always prefer enlisting over battling cubes of that color. Save your battles for tribes you have not yet allied with, and even then, only fight when the cube count is becoming dangerous.

walkthrough.title: Role Synergies
walkthrough.text: Some role combinations work better together than others. The Consul excels at forging alliances because their ability helps gather cards. Pair them with a player focused on card collection. The Praefectus Classis is a naval specialist who can move quickly between ports, making them ideal for managing threats across the map. Roles that boost combat pair well with roles that boost recruitment. Discuss who covers which responsibilities based on your role matchup at the start of the game. Specialization is more effective than having everyone try to do everything.

walkthrough.title: Managing the Barbarian Deck
walkthrough.text: After an Intensify shuffle, the discard pile goes on top of the Barbarian deck. This means you have perfect information about the top portion of the deck. Count the discarded cards before Intensify. Those cities will be invaded again in the near future. Place your defenses accordingly. Once you draw through the shuffled portion, the deck becomes unpredictable again. Experienced players mentally track which Barbarian cards have been seen and which remain unseen to estimate invasion probabilities.

walkthrough.title: Defensive Positioning on Migration Paths
walkthrough.text: Each tribe's migration path has entry points at the map edge and progresses inward toward Roma. If you can hold a fortified city early on a migration path with legions present, barbarian cubes will be absorbed by defense before they can push deeper. This creates a chokepoint. The barbarian cube is not placed and you only lose one legion, which you can replenish by recruiting. This is far more sustainable than trying to clear cubes after they have spread across the interior. Think of it as holding the frontier rather than chasing fires.

walkthrough.title: Revolt Timing Awareness
walkthrough.text: Revolt cards are distributed through the Player deck in roughly equal intervals. After you see one, you have a rough idea of when the next will appear. Use the calm periods between Revolts to build up your defenses, forge alliances, and clear cubes. When you sense a Revolt is imminent, ensure no city has three cubes of the same color because the Intensify shuffle guarantees those cities will be hit again. Preventive cube clearing before an expected Revolt is one of the strongest advanced plays.

walkthrough.title: Balancing Offense and Defense
walkthrough.text: The hardest strategic decision in Fall of Rome is when to push for alliances versus when to play defense. If you focus entirely on collecting cards and forging alliances, the board will be overrun with cubes while you are not fighting. If you focus entirely on battling barbarians, you will never gather enough cards to forge alliances and will eventually be overwhelmed by the invasion rate increase. The balance shifts as the game progresses. Early game, favor defense and fortification. Mid game, shift toward card collection and alliance building. Late game, use Enlist Barbarians aggressively to clean up remaining cubes.

walkthrough.title: The Legion Economy
walkthrough.text: Sixteen legions sounds like a lot, but they deplete fast. Every battle costs legions. Every ambush wipes them out. Every defense costs one. Advanced players track the legion supply obsessively. If you have fewer than four legions in the supply, stop battling unless absolutely necessary and focus on recruiting. A key insight: enlisting allied barbarians adds legions from the supply without spending a Recruit action, effectively doubling your legion generation. This is why early alliances are so powerful — they unlock a second source of soldiers.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Win Conditions
walkthrough.text: As you approach victory, remember that every tribe must be either allied or eliminated. Eliminated means zero cubes of that color on the entire board. For tribes you have allied with, cubes still appear during invasions, so you need to enlist them continuously or time your final cleanup. A strong endgame involves having three or four tribes allied and using Enlist Barbarians each turn to clear their cubes, while the final tribe is being eliminated through concentrated combat. Do not try to eliminate all five tribes through combat alone — it is nearly impossible against the escalating invasion rate.

summary.title: Optimal Fortification Placement
bullets: Fortify cities on multiple migration paths with high recruitment values
Avoid dead-end edge cities — forts are launch points, not walls
Connect to 3+ adjacent cities for flexible response

summary.title: Alliance Priority Order
bullets: Ostrogoths first (3 cards), then Huns/Visigoths (4), then Anglo-Saxons/Vandals (5)
Each alliance unlocks Enlist Barbarians for that color
Cheapest first unless a tribe is about to overwhelm the board

summary.title: When to Battle vs When to Enlist
bullets: Battle: non-allied tribes, low cube counts, plenty of legions
Enlist: always prefer for allied tribes (1 card removes all cubes + gains legions)
3 dice average: ~1.5 cubes removed, ~1 legion lost

summary.title: Role Synergies
bullets: Pair card-collection roles with combat/recruitment roles
Naval specialists cover distant threats quickly
Specialize by role — don't have everyone doing the same thing

summary.title: Managing the Barbarian Deck
bullets: After Intensify: top of deck = recently discarded cards (known)
Count discards before Intensify to know the predictable window
Track seen vs unseen Barbarian cards for probability estimates

summary.title: Defensive Positioning on Migration Paths
bullets: Hold fortified cities early on migration paths as chokepoints
Defending absorbs cubes (1 legion lost, no cube placed)
Sustainable defense: recruit to replace lost defenders

summary.title: Revolt Timing Awareness
bullets: Revolt cards spaced through the deck at roughly equal intervals
Before an expected Revolt: clear any city with 3 same-color cubes
Use calm periods to build up, push alliances, and clear threats

summary.title: Balancing Offense and Defense
bullets: Early game: defense and fortification
Mid game: card collection and alliance building
Late game: Enlist Barbarians aggressively to clean up

summary.title: The Legion Economy
bullets: Track the supply: if below 4 legions, stop battling unless critical
Enlisting adds legions without a Recruit action
Early alliances = second source of soldiers = exponential advantage

summary.title: Endgame Win Conditions
bullets: Every tribe must be allied or eliminated (0 cubes on board)
Allied tribes still invade — enlist continuously or time final cleanup
Ideal endgame: 3-4 tribes allied + enlist, 1 tribe eliminated by combat

entries.definition: Role. The Consul specializes in diplomacy. Their special ability makes it easier to gather cards for alliances by reducing the cost or improving card exchange. Ideal for the player focused on forging alliances.

Role. The Magister Militum is the supreme military commander. Their ability enhances combat effectiveness, such as rerolling dice or gaining extra dice in battle. Best for the player leading the fight against barbarians.

Role. The Praefectus Classis is the fleet commander. Their ability enhances sailing and naval movement, allowing faster travel between port cities without discarding cards or with reduced cost. Best for responding to distant threats.
Role. The Regina has political influence across the Empire. Their ability allows special card manipulation or movement bonuses that help coordinate with other players. A versatile support role.

Role. The Mercator is the merchant. Their ability involves resource manipulation, such as drawing extra cards, trading cards more freely, or using cards in unconventional ways. Good for building card sets quickly.

Role. The Praefectus Fabrum is the chief engineer. Their ability enhances fortification and recruitment, such as building forts without discarding cards or recruiting additional legions. Essential for establishing the defensive network.
Role. The Vestalis is the guardian of sacred rites. Their ability provides defensive bonuses or the power to prevent sacking. A protective role that helps the team survive critical moments.
Action. Move your pawn to an adjacent city connected by a road. You may bring up to 3 legions from your current city with you. Costs 1 action. Can be repeated multiple times per turn.

Action. Move your pawn between two port cities (marked with anchor symbols) by discarding a card matching your current city or destination. You may bring legions with you. Allows rapid cross-map movement at the cost of a card.
Action. Discard a card matching your current city to place a fort token there. Maximum 1 fort per city, 6 forts total in the game. Forts enable recruiting and protect legions from ambush.
Action. Add legions from the supply equal to the city's recruitment value. You must be in a city with a fort. There are 16 legions total. If the supply is empty, you cannot recruit.
Action. Roll up to 1 die per legion in your city, maximum 3 dice. Dice results determine barbarians removed and legions lost. Combat is the primary way to remove cubes of non-allied tribes.
Action. Give or take a Player card with another player who is in the same city as you. The card exchanged must match the city both players occupy. Used to gather matching cards for alliances.

Action. Discard a set of Player cards matching one tribe's color to forge an alliance. Card costs: Anglo-Saxons/Franks 5, Vandals 5, Huns 4, Visigoths 4, Ostrogoths 3. Flip that tribe's Alliance card to allied side.

Action. Available only for allied tribes. Discard 1 card of an allied tribe's color while in a city with cubes of that color. Remove all matching cubes from the city and add 1 legion per cube removed from the supply.
Barbarian tribe (orange cubes). Invade from the northwest along their migration path. Alliance cost: 5 matching cards. One of the more expensive tribes to ally with.
Barbarian tribe (black cubes). Invade from the south and southwest along their migration path. Alliance cost: 5 matching cards. Threaten the southern coast and can pressure Roma from below.
Barbarian tribe (green cubes). Invade from the east along their migration path. Alliance cost: 4 matching cards. Known for deep penetration into the Empire's interior.
Barbarian tribe (yellow cubes). Invade from the southeast along their migration path. Alliance cost: 4 matching cards. Their path often threatens cities near Roma.
Barbarian tribe (blue cubes). Invade from the northeast along their migration path. Alliance cost: 3 matching cards. The cheapest alliance, making them the ideal first target for diplomacy.
Battle die result. You lose 1 legion (returned to supply). No barbarian cubes are removed. The worst possible result.
Battle die result. Remove 1 barbarian cube from the city. No legions are lost. Appears on 2 faces of the die. The best standard result.
Battle die result. Remove 1 barbarian cube AND lose 1 legion. A trade of one-for-one.
Battle die result. Remove 2 barbarian cubes and lose 1 legion. The best possible result — you clear two barbarians for the cost of one soldier.
Battle die result. Triggers the special combat ability of your role card. Effect varies by role. Check your role card for specifics.
A Player card showing a specific city and its tribe color. Used for sailing, fortifying, plotting, and forging alliances. The backbone of the Player deck.
A Player card with a special one-time effect. Can be played on any player's turn. Provides powerful benefits like moving legions, removing cubes, or drawing cards.
An Event card printed upside-down with two options: a standard beneficial effect and a more powerful corrupt effect that comes with a negative consequence. You choose which version to use.

A special card shuffled into the Player deck. When drawn, triggers three effects in order: increase invasion rate, revolt the bottom Barbarian discard card (place 3 cubes), and Intensify (shuffle discard pile on top of Barbarian deck).
A card from the Barbarian deck showing a city on a tribe's migration path. Flipped during the Invade phase. Determines where barbarian cubes are placed.
One of 5 cards (one per tribe) placed near the board. Flipped to allied side when that tribe's alliance is forged. Tracks which tribes have been diplomatically resolved.

A wooden token placed in a city via the Fortify action. Maximum 1 per city, 6 total in game. Roma starts with 1. Forts enable the Recruit Army action and protect legions from ambush (attacked instead). Removed when a city is sacked.

A Roman soldier token. 16 total in the supply. Moved with player pawns via March or Sail. Used for battle (1 die per legion, max 3). Lost in battle, defense, or ambush. Gained through recruiting or enlisting allied barbarians.

Colored wooden cubes representing barbarian warriors. 5 colors matching the 5 tribes. Placed during invasion and revolt. Removed by battle, enlisting, or event cards. 4th same-color cube in a city triggers sacking.

3 custom dice used for the Battle action. Faces include: legion (lose 1), barbarian (remove 1), combined (remove 1 + lose 1), double remove (remove 2 + lose 1), and role special. Roll up to 1 per legion, max 3.

Your character token on the board. Moves via March and Sail. Your presence (pawn) in a city with legions means those legions are attacked rather than ambushed when barbarians invade. Each player has one pawn.

A sequence of cities that each barbarian tribe follows when invading. Cubes can only be placed if the target city or a city earlier on the path already has cubes of that color. If neither has cubes, the cube goes to the path's starting city. Each of the 5 tribes has its own unique migration path printed on the board.
When a barbarian cube would be placed in a city with legions AND either a fort or a player pawn: 1 legion is removed and the cube is NOT placed. The defense succeeds. This is sustainable defense.

When a barbarian cube would be placed in a city with legions but NO fort and NO player pawn: ALL legions in the city are destroyed and the cube IS placed. Devastating loss. Always protect legions with a fort or your presence.

Triggered when a 4th cube of the same color would be placed in a city. The cube is not placed. Instead: Decline marker drops 1, fort is removed (if present), and 1 cube of that color spreads to each adjacent city. Can cause chain reactions if adjacent cities also reach 4.

When sacking spreads cubes to adjacent cities and one of those cities also reaches 4 same-color cubes, that city sacks too. This can cascade through multiple cities in a single invasion, dropping the Decline track rapidly. Preventing chain reactions is a top strategic priority.

The third step of a Revolt card. Shuffle the entire Barbarian discard pile and place it on top of the Barbarian deck. This guarantees recently invaded cities will be invaded again soon. Creates a window of predictable invasions.

The number of Barbarian cards flipped during the Invade phase each turn. Starts at 2 and increases by 1 each time a Revolt card is drawn. Tracked on the Invasion Rate track on the board. Higher rate = more barbarian pressure per turn.

A track on the board counting how many cities have been sacked. Starts at the top and drops 1 per sacking event. If it reaches the final space (8 sacks), you lose the game immediately. Chain sacking can drop it multiple steps at once.

Port cities are marked with an anchor symbol on the board. You can Sail between any two port cities by discarding a card matching either the origin or destination city. Sailing lets you cross the map quickly but costs a card.
Each player may hold a maximum of 7 Player cards. If you exceed 7 at the end of the Draw phase, you must discard down to 7. Plan your card usage to avoid wasteful discards.
The main draw deck containing City cards, Event cards, and Revolt cards. Players draw 2 cards from it each turn after taking actions. When the deck runs out and you need to draw, you lose the game.

The invasion deck containing cards for each city on the migration paths. Cards are flipped during the Invade phase equal to the invasion rate. Discarded cards can be shuffled back on top via Intensify from Revolt cards.
